speaker: interviewer
text: Milly, you kept that dog for a long time. What was its name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Its name was Tommy. What else? And it was such an integral part of our family that after I had left home, I left the city. I went-- after I finished school, I went to do nurses' training. One day, I get a call while I'm at work in another town from my brother. And he's in tears. And he says, you have to come home immediately.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I thought one of my parents had gotten killed. And I said, what happened? He said, Tommy's dead. Tommy got hit by a car. You have to come. And there was no question about it, I went home.

speaker: interviewee
text: After I was through with-- I think-- I don't think I went to class that night. I hopped on a train. And I went home, because Tommy had died. It was part of the family. It-- he recognized my father's car, blocks before my dad would be home. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: So tell me now, the, the Canadians came.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewer
text: You now had Tommy. And you had each other. What happened then?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had to start from scratch. Everything was gone.

speaker: interviewer
text: You went back to your town?

speaker: interviewee
text: We, we went back to our town. But there was very left-- very little left. Our house had been bombed. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My father's merchandise had been taken by the Germans. It had been taken by the police. And the police had cooperated with the Germans, so there was nothing. I had cousins, first cousins, children of my father's oldest brother who came back from the war, but their parents didn't. So they became part of our household. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We lived on the farm with the Roerink family for a while, till we were-- because there was a housing shortness for, for years. And then we were given a house. And my grandparents came back. They had nothing left. My grandmother was with us. And it was all my father and mother who were responsible. And they did whatever they could. My father started-- I remember he started working with camp survivors that came back to Holland. He worked for the Jewish-- whatever was left of-- I don't know-- of the Jewish community in Enschede. I think it was somehow HIAS was tied in at that point. And I was allowed to go with him at one point. And, of course, that was the first time that I saw camp survivors. And this was a group of women. And this will be with me until my dying day. They all just grabbed at me. Here was a child that had, that survived, that didn't have her hair shaven, that they could talk to, that-- they all wanted to touch me, to be-- have part of me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And somehow, I think at that point, I felt I was-- who am I? I was only in hiding. I did not survive the camp. I did not go through and, through what others who came after this first group that I met had gone through. So did you feel lucky or did feel ashamed? [INAUDIBLE] guilty?

speaker: interviewee
text: I felt, I felt guilty. I felt guilty. Why me? Why not the others? Where were my playmates? Where were so many of my cousins? [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: They had been-- had they-- when they were in hiding, some of them were found. Others never made it to hiding. They were picked up and sent to concentration camp and didn't survive. There was that guilt and that question, why? What did you do with that question?

speaker: interviewee
text: It took many years. It took many years. And it, it stayed inside of me. I, I couldn't talk about it because there was nobody who allowed this discussion to take place. It wasn't really until the late '80s, when I heard of the child survivors in Los Angeles, and talked to Sarah Moskovitz, and said, but I was only in hiding, who at that point says, this is the last time you say only. Because you suffered. You never knew from one minute to the next what would happen. You were-- you had a lot taken away from you. And I think that thought and meeting my sibling child survivors has given me a degree of peace and a degree of, I'm, I'm OK. I'm all right. I'm not the only one. There are others like me. So it's, it's not that unusual. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There were hard times before-- after the war and before I came here-- very hard times. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My parents decided to go to the United States. This was in the early '50s. And we all thought we, we might be able to make a new beginning. And my parents applied for the papers. And my father, by coincidence, had been born in Germany, fell under the German quota, which hardly anybody was eligible for. So that went a little faster than the Dutch quota. It still took over two years. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The passports and the papers came in in, on July 4th. On June 27, I had turned 21. I was of age and a Dutch citizen. And my papers were cancelled. I could not join them. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And again, it was, you don't belong. You're not good enough. And at that point, my parents, who had made all their plans to go to California, said, we don't want to leave you behind. It's your decision. And I said, no. You go, I'll come. It will be a few months, and I'll follow. Well, the few months stretched into two and 1/2 years that I was by myself in Holland. And, again, there was that separation and the, the pain, the, the homesickness, the agony. But in '55, I made it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And by then, you were a nurse.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was a nurse. I was a trained nurse. And I went to work here, didn't like it at all. The nursing in the United States was totally different from the way I had been taught. I mean, I still had this Florence Nightingale image [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that wasn't there. And I met a very sympathetic Jewish physician here, who said, Milly, this is not what you want to do. Go back to school, re-train. I did. I went back to school. I trained. I took classes in radiology, lab procedures, medical technology, et cetera, et cetera. And from then on, I worked as a nurse in a, in private doctors' practices, which I loved, very satisfactory. In the meantime, I had met George. We got married, had our child in '62. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did George do?

speaker: interviewee
text: George taught. George was a high school teacher. And George had lived in Holland. But I had, of course, never met him. He had come to the United States in the early '40s. But there is a wonderful bond besides our mutual attraction and love for each other, of being-- both being able to speak about Holland and one knowing what the other is talking about. And George has great understanding and has put up with a lot of my anxieties and nightmares. And he's there to, to, to hold and comfort me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Milly, what stayed with you? Talk about that. What about those anxieties? What are they about?

speaker: interviewee
text: I usually don't remember my nightmares when I wake up. I sometimes don't even wake up. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Or I will, will wake up while George is holding me. The-- there is-- there is-- when I do remember something, there is this, this feeling of running. I'm running. I'm running away. And I can't run fast enough. They're after me. It has gotten less, though. George tells me that I scream less often. And there were-- before that, there were times where we thought, well, one of these days, the neighbors will call the police and say something is going on in that house, because we hear this woman scream. But that has not happened. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] My parents resettled. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We had difficult years. We lost my brother in 1968. He had a massive heart attack. And he was a strong, strapping, 31-year-old who seemed in perfect health. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] He must have had heart damage. We had-- he was extremely ill during our hiding time, I came down with scarlet fever, with a mild case, gave it to him. He was dreadfully ill. There were no juices. There were, there were no liquids, but water and some milk, no physician, no medication, nothing. Deep in my heart, I think that that may have had played part, that that could have been the cause of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he under great tension since the war? Was he frightened easily, and--

speaker: interviewee
text: I, he didn't seem to be. He went on, He came here as a 16-year-old, went to college, started his own firm. He became an electrical engineer, developed a, an electronic gadget on which he was trying to get a--

speaker: interviewer
text: Patent.

speaker: interviewee
text: A patent, would have gone far. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We were very close. And I desperately miss him still.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your father do here?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father had a bicycle shop, right here in Pacific Palisades, and-- which, I guess, is the logical thing to do for a Dutchman-- and did very well. It was very popular. He had a wonderful way with people, a wonderful way with kids. It was next door to an elementary school. And any child who would forget his lunch money, there's no question, he'd go to Leo. Leo would take care of him. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] My parents, obviously, never got over their loss. But they were able to retire and have a good retirement. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They made friends. They made-- they took trips.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your father ever write anything about the Holocaust or his experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, he didn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he ever talk about--

speaker: interviewee
text: The only time he spoke about it was in front of his congregation. He-- my parents became-- after their retirement-- very active in their congregation. And my father was president for a couple of years. And on Yom HaShoah, he gave-- two years-- he gave a speech. And those, I have on tape. But as far as talking about anything to any of us, we-- my son-in-law, who is a writer, tried to get him to talk, tried to interview him. He wouldn't. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: He was quite a hero.

speaker: interviewee
text: He really was. He really was. He certainly was my hero. He was quite a guy.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like to meet your present hero.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I would love for you to meet my present hero.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's get George here.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. Good, so now George is here. George, you are the big hero here.

speaker: interviewee
text: Is that right? [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, that's right. Tell me how life has been different for the two of you because of your wife's past experiences in the Holocaust.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's a very difficult question to answer, because you can't split yourself in half and say, gee, if it had been different, the Holocaust hadn't occurred, it might have been, you know, different.

speaker: interviewer
text: How much of a shadow has it cast on your lives?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it's daily.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me.

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean, we talk about things in term-- I mean, I don't know. It's, it's-- to me, I think about it a lot, and sometimes makes me very angry. And sometimes, that overshadows things. I get short-tempered. And I think it's all, perhaps, traceable back to some very frustrating times.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were also in Holland. But when did you live there?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were very fortunate. We lived there from 1933 to '39.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we escaped to England, and from England, got to the United States in 1940.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I wasn't there during the war time. But I always say, we were on the run from 1933 on, and never stopped. And so I think our experiences have been influenced. I don't think that we really know how different it might have been if there hadn't been a Holocaust.

speaker: interviewer
text: George, when Milly has her nightmares, do you-- does she ever talk about what they were, or do you hear her? Do you know what her nightmares are about?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. She wakes up. And it used to be worse than it is. But when I-- we go to bed at different times. I get-- I stay up a little late sometimes. And when I come in the bedroom, oh, just horrendous scream. Something has happened. And Milly's very upset. And that lasts for a few minutes. But, no, we don't discuss it. The reason I don't, I've always felt that it must be extremely painful. And therefore, I just don't delve into it.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you're there for her.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, he is.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm always there for her. And I think that kind of binds us together, too, the fact that we do share a common experience.

speaker: interviewer
text: How much have you told your daughter about the Holocaust? Was she interested? Oh, I think she's here.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah, she's there, she-- yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: You're here at a perfect time, Michelle. Come.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hi.

speaker: interviewee
text: Will you tell the story of the [INAUDIBLE]?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: Michelle.

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: We were just talking about how-- I was about to ask how much you knew about the story of your mother's experiences during the Holocaust.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think quite a bit, but in bits and pieces over the years, rather than being sat down at one point and being told the whole story.

speaker: interviewer
text: I read a paper that you had written about the Dutch people during the Holocaust, and how they'd helped, and how-- and what methods the Jews had used to, to hide. It was very interesting.

speaker: interviewee
text: Was it-- you're talking about a paper I haven't seen in 10 years. [LAUGHTER] So I have no recollection of it, but yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was the one that, I think, Grandma had.

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how has your life been different because your mom lived through this terrible time?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's rather hard to say, of course, since this was the only life I led. I mean, I think I'm probably a little more aware of the history than, say, my friends and people my age. I can recall a friend speaking about something in the 1940s, as a simpler time and thinking that-- and she was talking about something in the US. And it had nothing to do with the war. But it occurred to me that she, she wasn't thinking about Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, it just, it just wasn't in her awareness.

speaker: interviewer
text: But it was in yours.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you feel that you were protecting your mother, shielding her from bad experiences, when you were younger?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not particularly.

speaker: interviewer
text: No. So you felt her strength.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. Yeah. I don't think there was anything unusual in that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Milly?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What would you say to people 200 years from now, or perhaps your great, great grandchildren, if you had a chance to speak to them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mainly, remember this. Don't let this happen again. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Don't let this experience that we went through ever happen to others or to you again. Give people credit for who they are. Don't differentiate because they're Jew, Gentile, black, green, whatever. And do not forget our history. Remember-- remember us. Remember your Jewish background. I think that's-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] those roots, I think, are terribly important.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you, Milly.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's see some of your pictures now.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. I'm Milly Stern. And this is a four generation picture. It was taken in the early-- probably around 1934. It was taken in Holland, in my hometown of Enschede. And I think it was in our backyard. On the back row-- in the back row, on the left is my mother, Sinny Stofkooper. Next to her is her mother, Fanny Mayer. Then comes my great grandmother, whom I only knew as Oma Mayer. I really don't know what her first name was. I don't remember. And the toddler, that's, that's me. This is my maternal grandmother, Fanny Mayer. And it's a passport picture that was probably taken in the early 1950s. It was taken in Enschede, Holland. This is a picture of my paternal grandparents. And on the left is my grandmother. Her name is also Emilie-- I was named after her-- and my grandfather, Alex Stofkooper. And it must have been taken in the late '40s, early '50s. And it was in Enschede. And they were celebrating an anniversary, I suspect. They're wearing a flower. This is the wedding picture of my parents. This is my father, Leo Stofkooper, and his new bride, Sinny. It was taken in Enschede in 1931. This is my oldest uncle, my father's oldest brother, Isidore Stofkooper. It was taken in Holland. I-- he was a young man, so I suspect it was taken in the early 1920s. And he did not survive the Holocaust. This is Isidore's wife, my aunt Annie with their two children, my namesake, and cousin, also, Milly Stofkooper, and her brother, who was called Niels at that time. And he now calls himself Nathan. She lives in Holland, and he lives in Israel. And it was taken in Enschede. It looks like a park. And, again, it must have been mid-'30s. This is my brother, Lex, and me. Apparently, we were on a walk somewhere. I can't place it. It is in our hometown of Enschede. It was in the late 1930s, early 1940s. I don't think he is older than about four years old. This is Mrs. Sina Oosterfeld. And this was taken in a small town called Borne, in the late 1980s. I think she was celebrating her 80th birthday. And she is one of the people that saved my life. And she's a very, very special lady. These are Artyam and Hanna Roerink. This was taken in Usselo, near the town of Enschede. And it was taken in 1946. They, they rescued my family and hundreds of other Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they become righteous Christians?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This is the younger Mr. and Mrs. Roerink. This is Bernard and Anna Roerink with their two children, Heda and Yoke. This was taken also in Enschede. And this must have taken in the early 1950s. He was instrumental in not only saving my life and my family, but 1,200 other Jews. This is the whole family Roerink and the family Stofkooper, plus several other Jewish people that they had saved during the war. This was taken in Usselo in front of their barn. And it was taken in 1945 or 1946. It was shortly after the liberation.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you in the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I'm in the center somewhere. This is the last picture of my late brother, Alex Stofkooper. It was taken at his desk in the, in the San Fernando Valley. And it was in 1968.
